instruments.description: 50% duty cycle pulse wave. Smooth sustained tone. Attack: 0ms, Decay: 0ms, Sustain: 100%, Release: 0ms. Peak volume 127/127.

instrument_description.hardware: Pulse wave generator with 4 selectable duty cycles (12.5%, 25%, 50%, 75%). 11-bit period register sets frequency. Hardware envelope with 4-bit volume (0-15). Optional sweep unit for pitch bends.
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 0 for Pulse 1). CC11 controls per-frame volume. CC12 controls duty cycle (0-3 mapped to 12.5%-75%). The synth generates a bandlimited pulse wave at the specified duty ratio.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Warm, full, and round — a pure square wave. The richest harmonic content of any duty cycle. Most commonly used for lead melodies. Think: classic NES main theme sound. Consistent volume throughout the note with minimal decay. Creates a smooth, legato feel. Common in RPG town themes and slower pieces. ADSR: zero attack, minimal decay, high sustain. Average note duration: 1678ms (legato phrasing).
instrument_description.channel_role: Usually carries the melody (lead voice). In Capcom games, uses 50% duty for warm lead tone. In Konami games, often paired with Pulse 2 in call-and-response patterns.

instruments.description: 50% duty cycle pulse wave. Smooth sustained tone. Attack: 0ms, Decay: 0ms, Sustain: 100%, Release: 0ms. Peak volume 127/127.

instrument_description.hardware: Identical hardware to Square 1 — same duty cycles, period range, and envelope. The NES treats both pulse channels equally; the distinction is purely in how composers use them.
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 1 for Pulse 2). Same CC11/CC12 controls as Pulse 1. Typically assigned a different duty cycle than Pulse 1 for timbral contrast.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Warm, full, and round — a pure square wave. The richest harmonic content of any duty cycle. Most commonly used for lead melodies. instrument_description.channel_role: Harmony, countermelody, or echo. Capcom typically uses 25% duty (brighter/thinner than the 50% lead). Some games use Pulse 2 for arpeggiated chord fill or bass doubling.

instruments.description: Triangle wave bass. No volume control — only on/off gating. One octave below pulse for the same period value. Average note duration 447ms. Legato phrasing.

instrument_description.hardware: Fixed triangle waveform — no duty cycle control, no volume control (only on/off gating via the linear counter). Produces a frequency one octave LOWER than pulse channels for the same period value (32-step vs 16-step sequencer).
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 2 for Triangle). CC11 is always 127 (gate signal only). No CC12 (no duty cycle). Volume is either full or silent — articulation comes from note duration only.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Pure triangle wave bass. No timbre variation possible — all expression comes from note timing. Consistent volume throughout the note with minimal decay. Creates a smooth, legato feel. Common in RPG town themes and slower pieces. ADSR: zero attack, minimal decay, high sustain. Sustained bass lines. 47 notes captured.
instrument_description.channel_role: Bass. The only channel that can produce sub-bass frequencies. Staccato triangle = punchy bass hits. Legato triangle = sustained bass lines. The lack of volume control makes it the 'loudest' channel in the mix.

instruments.description: Noise channel percussion. 16 hits in the capture. Sparse percussion.

instrument_description.hardware: Linear-feedback shift register (LFSR) with 16 selectable periods and 2 modes (long = white noise, short = metallic/tonal). 4-bit volume with hardware envelope. No pitch in the traditional sense — period controls 'brightness'.
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 3 for Noise). Note number maps to drum sound (36=kick, 38=snare, 42=hi-hat). Velocity sets initial volume. ADSR envelope shapes the hit.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Percussion channel. 16 hits captured. Sparse accents. NES noise uses a shift register, not samples — every 'drum' is synthesized from filtered noise.
instrument_description.channel_role: Percussion. Low periods = hi-hats and cymbals. Mid periods = snare. High periods = kick drums. Some games use noise for wind/explosion effects. The short LFSR mode creates metallic tones used for distinctive 'chiptune snares'.

driver_description: Rare's custom NES drivers. Each game has its own engine — Battletoads uses a complex 20-command dispatch with dual-mode duration, while Wizards & Warriors uses a simpler 10-command format.
encoding_format: Byte index into period table. Notes ≥ $81, rest = $80, commands = $00-$7F. Duration is modal (set by command, persists). Software volume register with ramp/oscillate modes.
sonic_signature: Rich harmonies, moderate drums. Battletoads has the most complex command set of any NES driver we've analyzed (~20 opcodes).

driver_description: Capcom's internal NES sound engine, evolved across the Mega Man series (MM1-MM6) and Disney licensed titles. Known for extremely dense drum programming and bright pulse tones.
encoding_format: Octave + semitone byte. Full note identity in one byte — no separate octave command needed. Variable-width command dispatch.
sonic_signature: Bright, driving, rhythmic. Heavy noise channel usage (often 700+ hits per 80 seconds). Dual-duty pulse arrangement: 50% lead, 25% harmony. The most 'band-like' sound on NES.

instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 0 for Pulse 1). CC11 controls per-frame volume. CC12 controls duty cycle (0-3 mapped to 12.5%-75%). The synth generates a bandlimited pulse wave at the specified duty ratio.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Warm, full, and round — a pure square wave. The richest harmonic content of any duty cycle. Most commonly used for lead melodies. Think: classic NES main theme sound. Natural fade from peak volume — like a plucked string. The most common envelope shape on NES. Gives notes a realistic quality. ADSR: zero attack, moderate decay, moderate sustain. Average note duration: 473ms (legato phrasing).
instrument_description.channel_role: Usually carries the melody (lead voice). In Capcom games, uses 50% duty for warm lead tone. In Konami games, often paired with Pulse 2 in call-and-response patterns.

instruments.description: 50% duty cycle pulse wave. Natural decay. Attack: 0ms, Decay: 50ms, Sustain: 61%, Release: 17ms. Peak volume 59/127.

instrument_description.hardware: Identical hardware to Square 1 — same duty cycles, period range, and envelope. The NES treats both pulse channels equally; the distinction is purely in how composers use them.

instruments.description: Triangle wave bass. No volume control — only on/off gating. One octave below pulse for the same period value. Average note duration 68ms. Staccato articulation.

instrument_description.hardware: Fixed triangle waveform — no duty cycle control, no volume control (only on/off gating via the linear counter). Produces a frequency one octave LOWER than pulse channels for the same period value (32-step vs 16-step sequencer).
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 2 for Triangle). CC11 is always 127 (gate signal only). No CC12 (no duty cycle). Volume is either full or silent — articulation comes from note duration only.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Pure triangle wave bass. No timbre variation possible — all expression comes from note timing. Consistent volume throughout the note with minimal decay. Creates a smooth, legato feel. Common in RPG town themes and slower pieces. ADSR: zero attack, minimal decay, high sustain. Staccato bass hits. 1063 notes captured.
instrument_description.channel_role: Bass. The only channel that can produce sub-bass frequencies. Staccato triangle = punchy bass hits. Legato triangle = sustained bass lines. The lack of volume control makes it the 'loudest' channel in the mix.

instruments.description: Noise channel percussion. 9 hits in the capture. Sparse percussion.

instrument_description.channel_role: Percussion. Low periods = hi-hats and cymbals. Mid periods = snare. High periods = kick drums. Some games use noise for wind/explosion effects. The short LFSR mode creates metallic tones used for distinctive 'chiptune snares'.

driver_description: David Warhol's driver for Realtime Associates (1990-1992). Composers created MIDI files in Cakewalk/Performer, which Warhol arranged and converted to NES format. Never uses DPCM.
encoding_format: MIDI-derived text format. Standard NTSC period table spanning 7 octaves. Entry points: $8029 = play_song(X), $80A0 = per-frame tick.
sonic_signature: The 'echo' instrument: hardware sweep every 13 frames creating reverb-like decay. Duty cycle basses. Square channel drums (especially in Defenders of Dynatron City).

driver_description: Konami's workhorse NES driver, used across dozens of titles. Notes are nibble-packed: high 4 bits = pitch (C-B), low 4 bits = duration index. DX commands configure envelope and tempo per section.
encoding_format: Nibble-packed: [PPPP DDDD] where P=pitch, D=duration index. Duration = tempo × (D+1) frames.

driver_description: Capcom's internal NES sound engine, evolved across the Mega Man series (MM1-MM6) and Disney licensed titles. Known for extremely dense drum programming and bright pulse tones.

driver_description: David Warhol's driver for Realtime Associates (1990-1992). Composers created MIDI files in Cakewalk/Performer, which Warhol arranged and converted to NES format. Never uses DPCM.

sonic_signature: The 'echo' instrument: hardware sweep every 13 frames creating reverb-like decay. Duty cycle basses. Square channel drums (especially in Defenders of Dynatron City).

sonic_signature: Unpredictable and game-specific. Ranges from Kirby's lush four-channel arrangements to Metroid's sparse, atmospheric drones.

instrument_description.sonic_character: Warm, full, and round — a pure square wave. The richest harmonic content of any duty cycle. Most commonly used for lead melodies. driver_description: Capcom's internal NES sound engine, evolved across the Mega Man series (MM1-MM6) and Disney licensed titles. Known for extremely dense drum programming and bright pulse tones.
encoding_format: Octave + semitone byte. Full note identity in one byte — no separate octave command needed. Variable-width command dispatch.
sonic_signature: Bright, driving, rhythmic. Heavy noise channel usage (often 700+ hits per 80 seconds). Dual-duty pulse arrangement: 50% lead, 25% harmony. The most 'band-like' sound on NES.

driver_description: Nintendo's first-party sound engines vary significantly per game. Some use data-driven players, others use code-as-music (songs as 6502 subroutines). Kid Icarus has a unique dual-engine architecture.
encoding_format: Varies by game. Kid Icarus: code-as-music + $AC88 config table. Kirby: data-driven with MMC3 banking. Early games (Ice Climber, DK): simple NROM drivers.
sonic_signature: Unpredictable and game-specific. Ranges from Kirby's lush four-channel arrangements to Metroid's sparse, atmospheric drones.

instruments.description: Noise channel percussion. 443 hits in the capture. Dense drum pattern.

instrument_description.hardware: Linear-feedback shift register (LFSR) with 16 selectable periods and 2 modes (long = white noise, short = metallic/tonal). 4-bit volume with hardware envelope. No pitch in the traditional sense — period controls 'brightness'.
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 3 for Noise). Note number maps to drum sound (36=kick, 38=snare, 42=hi-hat). Velocity sets initial volume. ADSR envelope shapes the hit.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Percussion channel. 443 hits captured. Dense drum pattern — continuous beats. NES noise uses a shift register, not samples — every 'drum' is synthesized from filtered noise.
instrument_description.channel_role: Percussion. Low periods = hi-hats and cymbals. Mid periods = snare. High periods = kick drums. Some games use noise for wind/explosion effects. The short LFSR mode creates metallic tones used for distinctive 'chiptune snares'.

driver_description: Square's NES sound engine for the Final Fantasy series. Music data starts at ROM $34010 with a Master Music Table of 23 songs × 3 channel pointers. MIDI-to-text conversion workflow.
encoding_format: First digit (0-B) = note (C through B). Second digit = duration (0=longest, 9=shortest). $D8-$DB = octave shift. $F = control string (tempo, instrument). $D0/$D1 = loop/repeat.
sonic_signature: Classical, arpeggiated. The Prelude's ascending arpeggio is the most recognizable NES musical motif. Minimal percussion — relies on pulse interplay for rhythm.

driver_description: Square's NES sound engine for the Final Fantasy series. Music data starts at ROM $34010 with a Master Music Table of 23 songs × 3 channel pointers. MIDI-to-text conversion workflow.
encoding_format: First digit (0-B) = note (C through B). Second digit = duration (0=longest, 9=shortest). $D8-$DB = octave shift. $F = control string (tempo, instrument). $D0/$D1 = loop/repeat.
sonic_signature: Classical, arpeggiated. The Prelude's ascending arpeggio is the most recognizable NES musical motif. Minimal percussion — relies on pulse interplay for rhythm.

instrument_description.sonic_character: Pure triangle wave bass. No timbre variation possible — all expression comes from note timing. Consistent volume throughout the note with minimal decay. Creates a smooth, legato feel. Common in RPG town themes and slower pieces. ADSR: zero attack, minimal decay, high sustain. Sustained bass lines. 13 notes captured.
instrument_description.channel_role: Bass. The only channel that can produce sub-bass frequencies. Staccato triangle = punchy bass hits. Legato triangle = sustained bass lines. The lack of volume control makes it the 'loudest' channel in the mix.

instruments.description: Noise channel percussion. 30 hits in the capture. Sparse percussion.

instrument_description.hardware: Linear-feedback shift register (LFSR) with 16 selectable periods and 2 modes (long = white noise, short = metallic/tonal). 4-bit volume with hardware envelope. No pitch in the traditional sense — period controls 'brightness'.
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 3 for Noise). Note number maps to drum sound (36=kick, 38=snare, 42=hi-hat). Velocity sets initial volume. ADSR envelope shapes the hit.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Percussion channel. 30 hits captured. Sparse accents. NES noise uses a shift register, not samples — every 'drum' is synthesized from filtered noise.
instrument_description.channel_role: Percussion. Low periods = hi-hats and cymbals. Mid periods = snare. High periods = kick drums. Some games use noise for wind/explosion effects. The short LFSR mode creates metallic tones used for distinctive 'chiptune snares'.

driver_description: Konami's workhorse NES driver, used across dozens of titles. Notes are nibble-packed: high 4 bits = pitch (C-B), low 4 bits = duration index. DX commands configure envelope and tempo per section.
encoding_format: Nibble-packed: [PPPP DDDD] where P=pitch, D=duration index. Duration = tempo × (D+1) frames.
sonic_signature: Dark, atmospheric sound. Parametric two-phase envelope (fast attack, controlled decay). Sparse but effective drum patterns.

driver_description: Nintendo's first-party sound engines vary significantly per game. Some use data-driven players, others use code-as-music (songs as 6502 subroutines). Kid Icarus has a unique dual-engine architecture.
encoding_format: Varies by game. Kid Icarus: code-as-music + $AC88 config table. Kirby: data-driven with MMC3 banking. Early games (Ice Climber, DK): simple NROM drivers.
sonic_signature: Unpredictable and game-specific. Ranges from Kirby's lush four-channel arrangements to Metroid's sparse, atmospheric drones.

driver_description: Nintendo's first-party sound engines vary significantly per game. Some use data-driven players, others use code-as-music (songs as 6502 subroutines). Kid Icarus has a unique dual-engine architecture.
encoding_format: Varies by game. Kid Icarus: code-as-music + $AC88 config table. Kirby: data-driven with MMC3 banking. Early games (Ice Climber, DK): simple NROM drivers.
sonic_signature: Unpredictable and game-specific. Ranges from Kirby's lush four-channel arrangements to Metroid's sparse, atmospheric drones.

instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 0 for Pulse 1). CC11 controls per-frame volume. CC12 controls duty cycle (0-3 mapped to 12.5%-75%). The synth generates a bandlimited pulse wave at the specified duty ratio.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Thin, nasal, almost buzzy. The narrowest pulse width creates the brightest, most cutting tone. Used sparingly — often for attack transients or special effects. Think: chiptune 'sting' or harpsichord-like pluck. Natural fade from peak volume — like a plucked string. The most common envelope shape on NES. Gives notes a realistic quality. ADSR: zero attack, moderate decay, moderate sustain. Average note duration: 350ms (legato phrasing).
instrument_description.channel_role: Usually carries the melody (lead voice). In Capcom games, uses 50% duty for warm lead tone. In Konami games, often paired with Pulse 2 in call-and-response patterns.

instruments.description: 50% duty cycle pulse wave. Sharp percussive attack. Attack: 0ms, Decay: 0ms, Sustain: 100%, Release: 0ms. Peak volume 85/127.

instrument_description.hardware: Identical hardware to Square 1 — same duty cycles, period range, and envelope. The NES treats both pulse channels equally; the distinction is purely in how composers use them.
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 1 for Pulse 2). Same CC11/CC12 controls as Pulse 1. Typically assigned a different duty cycle than Pulse 1 for timbral contrast.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Warm, full, and round — a pure square wave. The richest harmonic content of any duty cycle. Most commonly used for lead melodies. Think: classic NES main theme sound. Sharp attack with rapid decay — the note hits hard and fades quickly. Common for lead melodies in action games where each note needs to 'pop' out of the mix. ADSR: zero attack, fast decay, low sustain. Average note duration: 20ms (rapid staccato).
instrument_description.channel_role: Harmony, countermelody, or echo. Capcom typically uses 25% duty (brighter/thinner than the 50% lead). Some games use Pulse 2 for arpeggiated chord fill or bass doubling.

instruments.description: Triangle wave bass. No volume control — only on/off gating. One octave below pulse for the same period value. Average note duration 701ms. Legato phrasing.

instrument_description.hardware: Fixed triangle waveform — no duty cycle control, no volume control (only on/off gating via the linear counter). Produces a frequency one octave LOWER than pulse channels for the same period value (32-step vs 16-step sequencer).
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 2 for Triangle). CC11 is always 127 (gate signal only). No CC12 (no duty cycle). Volume is either full or silent — articulation comes from note duration only.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Pure triangle wave bass. No timbre variation possible — all expression comes from note timing. Consistent volume throughout the note with minimal decay. Creates a smooth, legato feel. Common in RPG town themes and slower pieces. ADSR: zero attack, minimal decay, high sustain. Sustained bass lines. 98 notes captured.
instrument_description.channel_role: Bass. The only channel that can produce sub-bass frequencies. Staccato triangle = punchy bass hits. Legato triangle = sustained bass lines. The lack of volume control makes it the 'loudest' channel in the mix.

instruments.description: Noise channel percussion. 140 hits in the capture. Moderate drum activity.

instrument_description.hardware: Linear-feedback shift register (LFSR) with 16 selectable periods and 2 modes (long = white noise, short = metallic/tonal). 4-bit volume with hardware envelope. No pitch in the traditional sense — period controls 'brightness'.
instrument_description.reaper_plugin: In ReapNES Console: Slider 33 = Channel Mode (set to 3 for Noise). Note number maps to drum sound (36=kick, 38=snare, 42=hi-hat). Velocity sets initial volume. ADSR envelope shapes the hit.
instrument_description.sonic_character: Percussion channel. 140 hits captured. Moderate percussion. NES noise uses a shift register, not samples — every 'drum' is synthesized from filtered noise.
instrument_description.channel_role: Percussion. Low periods = hi-hats and cymbals. Mid periods = snare. High periods = kick drums. Some games use noise for wind/explosion effects. The short LFSR mode creates metallic tones used for distinctive 'chiptune snares'.

driver_description: Nintendo's first-party sound engines vary significantly per game. Some use data-driven players, others use code-as-music (songs as 6502 subroutines). Kid Icarus has a unique dual-engine architecture.
encoding_format: Varies by game. Kid Icarus: code-as-music + $AC88 config table. Kirby: data-driven with MMC3 banking. Early games (Ice Climber, DK): simple NROM drivers.
sonic_signature: Unpredictable and game-specific. Ranges from Kirby's lush four-channel arrangements to Metroid's sparse, atmospheric drones.
